breastCancerRisk.title: Breast Cancer Risk Calculator
breastCancerRisk.description: Estimate your 5-year breast cancer risk with a simplified Gail model.
breastCancerRisk.elevatedHint: Above the Gail threshold of 1.66 % — discuss with a clinician.
breastCancerRisk.disclaimer: This estimate is not a substitute for medical advice. If your risk is elevated, talk to a gynecologist or breast clinic.
breastCancerRisk.howItWorksText: This calculator uses a simplified version of the Gail model (NCI Breast Cancer Risk Assessment Tool). It combines your risk factors into a relative risk and multiplies it by age-specific breast cancer incidence. A 5-year probability ≥ 1.66 % is considered elevated.
breastCancerRisk.effectFamily: One first-degree relative roughly doubles risk
breastCancerRisk.effectFirstBirth: First birth ≥ 30 or nulliparity raises risk
breastCancerRisk.effectAge: Risk rises nearly linearly with age up to ~75 years

faq.a: The Gail model (Gail et al. 1989) estimates individual 5-year and lifetime breast cancer risk from several risk factors — age, menarche, first birth, family history, and prior biopsies. It is the basis of the NCI Breast Cancer Risk Assessment Tool.

faq.q: When is my risk considered elevated?
faq.a: Guidelines (NCI, ASCO) define a 5-year risk ≥ 1.66 % as elevated. At that level you may be eligible for enhanced screening or risk-reducing medication.

Current age, age at menarche, age at first live birth or nulliparity, number of breast biopsies (with or without atypical hyperplasia), and breast cancer in first-degree relatives (mother, sister, daughter).
The Gail model underestimates risk for BRCA1/BRCA2 carriers and women with male relatives or second-degree relatives affected. Breast density, lifestyle, and hormone therapy are not included either.

At a population level, the Gail model is well calibrated (Costantino 1999, AUC ≈ 0.58–0.65). Individual prediction accuracy remains modest — treat the result as a conversation starter, not a diagnosis.

faq.q: What if my risk is elevated?
faq.a: If your 5-year risk is ≥ 1.66 %, ask your physician about enhanced screening (annual mammography ± MRI). At very high risk (e.g. ≥ 3 % over 5 years), preventive medication with tamoxifen or raloxifene may be discussed.
